title: Serverless-First Architecture
description: Structure the system as stateless, event-driven function handlers backed by managed services. No persistent servers — each function activates on demand, executes, and terminates. The architectural commitment is to build around events and cloud-managed primitives rather than long-running processes.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Serverless architectures have true pay-per-use pricing. Min=$0 represents development/staging environments with minimal traffic that stay within generous free tiers (AWS Lambda 1M free requests/month, API Gateway 1M calls/month first year, DynamoDB 25GB free). Max=$5000 represents a production workload with ~100M Lambda invocations/month ($20), API Gateway (~$350 for 100M requests), DynamoDB on-demand ($500-1000), S3 storage/transfer ($200-500), EventBridge ($100), Step Functions ($500), CloudWatch logs ($300), and data transfer costs ($1000-2000). High-traffic applications can exceed this, but $5000 covers most mid-scale serverless deployments processing millions of events monthly. Costs scale linearly with usage, making it cost-efficient for variable workloads but potentially expensive at very high scale compared to dedicated infrastructure.
provenance.adoptionCost: High complexity adoption requiring significant architectural shift. Team needs to learn event-driven design patterns, function composition, cold start optimization, distributed tracing, and stateless architecture principles. Implementation involves: refactoring existing code into functions (40-80 hours), setting up event buses and message routing (20-40 hours), implementing observability/monitoring for distributed functions (20-30 hours), configuring IAM/security policies (15-25 hours), setting up CI/CD pipelines for function deployment (15-25 hours), and handling distributed debugging/testing (20-30 hours). Requires expertise in cloud provider's serverless ecosystem (Lambda/Functions, EventBridge/EventGrid, Step Functions/Logic Apps). Initial productivity drop as team adapts to function-based development. Includes potential for architectural mistakes (over-fragmentation, chatty functions) requiring rework. Estimated 130-230 engineering hours at $150/hour blended rate = $3500 average.
provenance.licenseCost: Serverless services are consumption-based with no upfront licensing fees. AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions, API Gateway, EventBridge, and similar managed services charge only for usage with no license costs. Open-source frameworks like Serverless Framework, AWS SAM, or Terraform for infrastructure-as-code are free. Optional commercial tools exist (Serverless.com Pro/Enterprise for teams, Datadog/New Relic for enhanced monitoring) but aren't required. The pattern itself uses cloud-native managed services that bundle licensing into per-request pricing. Organizations may choose commercial observability tools ($500-2000/month) but these are operational costs, not adoption licenses.

provides.reasoning: Serverless-first architecture minimizes operational overhead by leveraging managed services and eliminating infrastructure management, which directly provides reduced operational burden.

Serverless functions automatically scale based on demand, from zero to high concurrency, which is a core characteristic of this pattern. This capability accurately represents the pattern's scaling behavior.
Serverless architecture is fundamentally event-driven, with functions triggered by various event sources such as HTTP requests, message queues, database changes, and scheduled events.
Serverless functions automatically scale up and down without manual intervention, handling varying loads from zero to high concurrency based on incoming requests and events.
The serverless pattern provides cost optimization through pay-per-execution pricing model, where costs are incurred only when functions are actually invoked, including scale-to-zero capability.
Serverless functions are inherently stateless, with each invocation being independent and ephemeral, which is a fundamental characteristic and constraint of this architectural pattern.
Serverless architecture pattern enables function-based compute without server management.
Serverless patterns rely on a managed API gateway to route HTTP requests to functions.

requires.reasoning: Serverless architecture depends entirely on cloud provider managed services (Lambda, API Gateway, managed databases, etc.). Without these managed services being available, the pattern cannot be implemented.
This pattern requires a FaaS platform (such as AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, or Google Cloud Functions) as the core compute service for executing serverless functions.
A managed API Gateway service is typically required to expose serverless functions via HTTP/REST APIs and handle request routing, authentication, and throttling.
Since serverless functions cannot maintain internal state between invocations, external services like managed databases, object storage, or caches are required for any state persistence needs.

supports_nfr.reason: Serverless platforms typically achieve up to 99.99% availability but not beyond due to managed service SLAs.
Serverless p95 latency floor is 50ms due to cold starts affecting a portion of requests.
Serverless p99 latency floor is 100ms due to cold starts disproportionately affecting tail latencies.
Serverless enables 1-5 minute RTO through stateless functions that auto-restart on failure.
Serverless functions scale elastically to handle up to 100K requests/second (360M jobs/hour) within platform concurrency limits.
At <60 jobs/hour (<1 per minute), serverless pay-per-invocation pricing and cold start overhead are inefficient compared to scheduled or always-on workers.
Serverless architectures handle up to 200K read QPS with elastic scaling but face platform concurrency ceilings.
Serverless write throughput ceiling at 100K QPS due to stateless coordination and downstream service limits.
Serverless provides comprehensive audit logging through platform services but can be disabled if not required.
Serverless functions can implement any tenant isolation strategy through managed databases and function-level partitioning.
Serverless architectures support PII handling with encryption, access controls, and compliance features regardless of PII presence.
Serverless supports GDPR through data residency controls, encryption at rest/transit, and deletion workflows.
Major serverless platforms are HIPAA-eligible with BAAs, encryption, and audit logging for healthcare data.
Serverless enables CCPA compliance through data access controls, deletion workflows, and consumer privacy tooling.
Serverless platforms support SOX compliance with immutable audit logs, change tracking, and access governance.
Serverless supports read-your-writes consistency through strongly consistent reads in managed databases like DynamoDB.
Serverless achieves strict durability through managed services like S3 (11 9s) and DynamoDB with synchronous replication.

supports_constraints.reason: The generic serverless pattern applies to cloud-agnostic specifications; use cloud-specific variants for provider-specific features.
Serverless platforms support all major programming languages with native runtimes or custom container support.
Serverless architecture supports all application platforms through event-driven functions and API gateways.
Serverless can leverage managed caching services like ElastiCache or Memorystore but does not require caching.
Serverless excels with event-driven async messaging patterns but also supports synchronous request-response workloads.
Serverless functions are ideal for on-demand AI inference workloads with scale-to-zero cost optimization.
Serverless supports multi-tenant architectures through function isolation and managed database partitioning.
Serverless functions work well for scheduled or event-triggered batch processing with elastic scaling.
Distributed transactions are challenging in serverless due to stateless, short-lived function execution and coordination overhead.
Serverless supports real-time streaming via Kinesis, Event Hubs, or Dataflow triggers for event processing.
Serverless functions can integrate with managed vector databases like Pinecone or Weaviate for AI/ML search.
Serverless integrates seamlessly with managed document databases like DynamoDB, Cosmos DB, or Firestore.
Serverless functions work well with managed key-value stores like DynamoDB, Redis, or Memcached for fast lookups.
Serverless functions can query managed graph databases like Neptune or Neo4j Aura for relationship-heavy workloads.
Serverless supports time-series workloads through managed services like Timestream, InfluxDB Cloud, or TimescaleDB.
OLTP workloads supported via serverless functions with managed databases like Aurora Serverless or Cosmos DB.
OLAP queries can trigger serverless functions though cold starts may impact latency for analytical workloads.

function_runtime_model.description: Runtime model: 'faas' for pure FaaS (Lambda/Cloud Functions), 'container_functions' for containerized serverless (Cloud Run/Fargate), 'hybrid' for mixing both based on workload needs

cold_start_optimization.description: Cold start strategy: 'minimal' accepts cold starts for cost savings, 'provisioned_concurrency' pre-warms instances for latency-sensitive workloads (higher cost), 'keep_warm' uses scheduled pings (moderate cost)

state_management.description: How to handle stateful workflows: 'external' uses databases/caches (most flexible), 'durable_functions' for orchestrated workflows with checkpointing, 'step_functions' for visual workflow orchestration

event_sources.description: Primary event trigger pattern: 'api_gateway' for synchronous HTTP APIs, 'event_bus' for async pub/sub events, 'stream_processing' for real-time data streams, 'multi_trigger' for diverse event sources

observability_level.description: Monitoring depth: 'standard' uses basic logs and metrics, 'enhanced' adds custom metrics and structured logging, 'distributed_tracing' implements full request tracing across functions (X-Ray, OpenTelemetry)

warn_nfr.reason: At very low QPS, serverless pay-per-use advantage is minimal and cold start overhead becomes significant relative to total requests.
warn_nfr.message: arch-serverless: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (<1). At this low volume, serverless cold start overhead may outweigh pay-per-use benefits; consider simpler hosting.
